summary: This post systematically evaluates the prediction accuracy of well-known futurists (Kurzweil, Kaku, Toffler, etc.) by grading their concrete predictions, finding accuracy rates of 3-10% for most futurists. The author contrasts these poor results with more accurate predictors who had deep domain expertise (like Steve Yegge and Bill Gates) and identifies common failure modes: cocktail-party level understanding, blindly extrapolating exponential trends, panacea thinking, and failure to learn from past errors. The key finding is that accurate prediction requires genuine domain expertise and probabilistic thinking, not broad theories or 'big ideas.'
key_points: Most prominent futurists had terrible accuracy rates (3-10%) despite claims of 86%+ accuracy through cherry-picking successful predictions

Common failure modes include: relying on exponential extrapolation without understanding mechanisms, 'rounding up' evidence optimistically, panacea thinking about technologies, and never learning from past errors
Accurate predictors like Steve Yegge (50% accuracy) and Bill Gates had deep domain expertise, access to real data flows, and critically evaluated their own past mistakes
The 'big ideas' approach (using a few theoretical hammers on everything) consistently failed across multiple futurists
High certainty about exponential scaling curves is particularly dangerous - small errors in growth rates compound massively over time
Superficial application of forecasting techniques (like adding probabilities after the fact) doesn't make predictions accurate without deep understanding
money_quotes: Fundamentally, the thing that derailed so many of Kurzweil's predictions is that he relied on the idea of exponential and accelerating growth in basically every area he can imagine
There's a sense in which it isn't too difficult to make correct predictions if you understand the topic and have access to the right data
Instead of doing what most futurists do and focusing on the predictions that worked out without looking at what went wrong isn't such a great way to improve
People who had (relatively) accurate predictions had a deep understanding of the problem and also tended to have a record of learning lessons from past predictive errors

If you take a pile of data, incorrectly interpret it and then round up the interpretation further to support a particular conclusion, throwing a probability on it at the end is not likely to make it accurate
key_insight: Accurate futurism requires deep domain expertise combined with rigorous self-criticism rather than broad theoretical frameworks - most celebrated futurists fail because they apply cocktail-party level understanding with unjustified certainty about exponential trends while never seriously evaluating their past errors.
